h: How to get good at this
b: Unit 7 ┬╖ Practice Labs Ear trainer The bridge between hearing music and playing it. Five minutes a day of interval and chord-quality recognition compounds faster than any other practice on this site. Intervals Triad quality Seventh chords ascending descending both at once έΨ╢ Play a question έΗ╗ Hear it again Press play to get a question. correct 0 / 0 streak 0 How to get good at this Start with the checked subset (thirds, fourth, fifth, octave) έΑΦ add intervals only when you're above ~80%. Replays are free. Listen as many times as you want; guessing fast teaches guessing. Sing before you answer. Matching the notes with your voice, even badly, is what wires them in έΑΦ the song-anchor table helps. Descending is its own skill. Most people learn ascending first and are surprised; train both.

Unit 7 ┬╖ Practice Labs Fretboard trainer Sixty seconds, one neck, no mercy. Name marked notes or find prompted ones έΑΦ misses become flashcards automatically, so the trainer and your review deck conspire against your weak spots. Name the note Find the note Frets 0έΑΥ5 Frets 0έΑΥ12 60-second round έΨ╢ Start round Name the marked note. Press start. score 0 streak 0 best έΑΦ time 60 How to use it Start on frets 0έΑΥ5, "name" mode. That region holds most of early bass life; get it under 2 seconds per note. "Find" mode is the inverse skill έΑΦ it's what you use when reading charts. Both directions matter. Think landmarks, not counting : the dots, the 5th-fret rule, octave shapes. If you're counting from the nut, slow down and re-anchor. Wrong answers feed your flashcards έΑΦ the notes that fool you will keep visiting until they don't.

Unit 7 ┬╖ Practice Labs Groove machine A patient band that never gets bored: pick a progression, a style, and a tempo έΑΦ then play along. The chart lights up, the fretboard shows the current chord's notes, and the whole thing loops until you stop. progression key style pattern tempo 90 custom chords (bars split by |) έΨ╢ Play count-in chord pads bass cues choose your bandέΑο Amber = the current chord's root, everywhere it lives; outlined = its other chord tones. Tap any fret to hear it. How to practice with it Match the cues first έΑΦ play exactly what the bass cues play until the pattern is in your hands, then turn "bass cues" off and own it alone. Roots-only is a complete workout : any progression, roots on beat 1, until the changes stop surprising you. Steal the styles : each Unit 6 page deep-links here with its groove pre-configured. Gap training : turn pads AND cues off έΑΦ just clicks and the highlighted chart. You are the harmony now. Presets to try Blues shuffle in A ┬╖ Pop-rock IέΑΥVέΑΥviέΑΥIV in C ┬╖ Walking iiέΑΥVέΑΥI in F ┬╖ Funk vamp on E7 ┬╖ One-drop doo-wop in G

Unit 7 ┬╖ Practice Labs Routine builder Tell it how long you've got; it assembles a session from the exercises you've been neglecting έΑΦ never-practiced first, then the stalest έΑΦ bookended by a warmup and something fun. Print it and put it on the music stand. 10 min 20 min 30 min 45 min Build my session ΏθΨρ Print it it reads your practice log and page progress έΑΦ nothing leaves your browser How it chooses Exercises from pages you've completed or visited come first έΑΦ the builder follows your path, it doesn't set one. Within those: never-practiced beats stale beats recent (it reads the "log practice" buttons you've been pressing). Every session starts with tuning + a hands warmup and ends with ears or free play έΑΦ the session shape from the technique unit.

Unit 7 ┬╖ Practice Labs Tuner Plug nothing in: this tuner listens through your device's microphone and shows exactly how far off each string is. (No mic? The reference pads below work by ear, like the tuning page taught.) ΏθΟν Enable microphone έΑΦ -50┬λ flat 0 +50┬λ sharp the meter needs your permission to listen έΑΦ or tune by ear with the pads below E 41.2 Hz A 55.0 Hz D 73.4 Hz G 98.0 Hz Getting a steady reading One string at a time , plucked over the end of the fingerboard (a rounder tone reads better), other strings muted. Let it ring έΑΦ the reading settles about half a second after the attack. Tune UP into the note (drop below, then come up) so the string holds its pitch. Within ┬▒5 cents the meter turns green έΑΦ that's in tune for any practical purpose. Why your phone can do this The tuner measures how often the waveform repeats (your low E repeats 41.2 times per second) by comparing the signal against time-shifted copies of itself έΑΦ the shift where they line up best IS the period. The same math from the fret-math page then turns frequency into the nearest note and the difference into cents (hundredths of a semitone).

h: The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 8 ┬╖ Expert Track Advanced techniques έΑΦ slap, tap, harmonics, and the fifth string The flashy wing of the vocabulary. Everything here is optional; everything here is fun; none of it substitutes for the pocket you built in Units 4έΑΥ6 έΑΦ it decorates it. Before you start Solid 16th-grid + ghost notes έΑΦ slap is 80% that skill wearing a leather jacket. Slap & pop, the honest primer The thumb (slap): strike the string with the side of the thumb's knuckle at the neck's end, bouncing OFF like a drumstick έΑΦ the fret-clack is the sound. Rotate from the forearm, loose wrist. The finger (pop): index slips under the D or G string and yanks it to snap against the frets. Thumb-pop pairs are funk's snare-and-kick. Ghosts still rule: slap lines are mostly muted thumb/pop on the grid; the pitched notes are the minority. If your funk ghosts are solid, slap is a tone change, not a new skill. Start at 60 bpm , thumb-only on one muted string, even as a metronome. Speed is compound interest on evenness. Tapping, harmonics, palm+thumb Tapping: fretting-hand hammer-ons taken to their conclusion έΑΦ both hands hammer notes on the fingerboard. Party trick until it's musical; the Unit 4 hammer-on drill is its foundation. Natural harmonics: touch (don't press) directly over fret 12, 7, or 5 and pluck έΑΦ bell tones. The 5th/7th-fret bells are also a tuning cross-check and the intro to "Portrait of Tracy". Palm mute + thumb: not advanced at all, but paired with these it completes the tonal palette έΑΦ thump, bell, clack, snap. The fifth string (and beyond) A 5-string adds a low B έΑΦ five more semitones of floor (B0 έΚΙ 30.9 Hz) and, just as valuable, positional reach : the notes under your hand span more range without shifting. Everything on this site transfers: the B string is a fourth below E, so every shape, interval, and rule works unchanged (the theory library on this site already knows the tuning). Real adjustments: right-hand muting gets stricter (a floating thumb helps έΑΦ the right-hand page fl
Two minutes of muted thumb-slaps on the E string at 60 bpm έΑΦ even as a drum machine, bouncing off the string, wrist loose enough to shake water off.
Thumb on the root (A, E-string 5έΑο any root), pop on its octave έΑΦ the classic pair, clean and even at 76 bpm, then in the disco-octave pattern from Unit 5.
Clear bells at frets 12, 7, and 5 on every string έΑΦ touch point exact, release immediate έΑΦ plus the E7/A5 tuning cross-check performed once.

h: The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 8 ┬╖ Expert Track Chord extensions & alterations C13έβψ11 looks like a password. It's actually a polite stack of thirds with two notes tweaked έΑΦ and the bassist's response to almost all of it is beautifully simple. Before you start Seventh chords and their symbols, cold. The idea Keep stacking thirds past the 7th and you get the extensions : the 9th (a 2nd an octave up), 11th (a 4th up high), 13th (a 6th up high). They add color, not function έΑΦ a C13 still behaves like a C7 that dressed up. Alterations bend extensions or the 5th by a semitone (έβφ9, έβψ9, έβψ11, έβφ13, έβφ5, έβψ5) έΑΦ mostly on dominant chords, to sharpen their pull. Reading the passwords Symbol Means Bassist's note C9 C7 + 9 root (the 9 is upstairs decor) Cmaj9 Cmaj7 + 9 root C13 C7 + 9 + 13 root; έβφ7 and 3 in fills C7έβφ9 / C7έβψ9 dominant with bent 9 root; expect a dark resolve C7alt dominant, everything bent (έβφ9 έβψ9 έβψ5έΑο) root, then get out of the way Cadd9 triad + 9, no 7th root; pop sparkle, not jazz tension C6/9 triad + 6 + 9 root; the "smiling ending" chord The liberating rule Numbers above 7 are the pianist's and guitarist's business. Your root (and 3rd/έβφ7 when you choose) supports every one of them. Never fight the upstairs έΑΦ a low 9th or 11th under the band turns color into mud. Big chords ask the bass for MORE simplicity, not less. Hear the stack grow C7 C9 C13 C7έβφ9 Same bass note under all four έΑΦ listen to the upstairs change while your job doesn't. The details (open as much as you like) Why the 11th is the troublemaker Over a major 3rd, the natural 11 sits a semitone above it έΑΦ the one rub that reads as error rather than color. Hence έβψ11 on major chords (the Lydia┬φn note) and plain 11 only on minor chords (where the έβφ3 leaves room). If you ever wondered why "sus" chords remove the 3rd: it's to make room for exactly this note. When the bass DOES play extensions High-register fills, chordal moments (solo bass, duos), and melodic hooks έΑΦ deliberately, upstairs, where they belong. The 9th two octaves above t
Ten scary symbols (Dm9, G13, Cmaj9, A7alt, F6/9, E7έβψ9, Bm11, Ab13, D7έβφ9, Gadd9) each decoded in seconds: root, family (maj/min/dom), and your first-choice note.

h: The method (four passes over any chart)
The common "visitors" (non-diatonic chords with day jobs)
The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 8 ┬╖ Expert Track Harmonic analysis έΑΦ reading the story Given any chord chart, you can now name the pieces. Analysis is naming the plot : which chords are home, which are travel, which are visitors from other keys έΑΦ so your line can tell the same story. Before you start Diatonic families , the circle , and Roman-numeral fluency. The method (four passes over any chart) Find the key έΑΦ key signature, first/last chords, and where things feel resolved. Write the numerals over the letters. Mark the functions : home (I, vi), departure (ii, IV), tension (V, vii┬░). Every progression is trips between these three rooms. Flag the visitors έΑΦ chords that don't fit the key (see below). They're the interesting bits. Find the bass story : strongest root motions (5ths falling), stepwise walks, pedal points. Your line should agree with the plot you just read. The common "visitors" (non-diatonic chords with day jobs) Visitor Example in C What it's doing Secondary dominant D7 έΗΤ G (written V7/V) borrowing V's pull to aim at a non-tonic chord Borrowed iv Fm in C major the bittersweet minor-plagal move (Beatles everywhere) έβφVII Bέβφ in C major the rock backdoor (borrowed from C minor) Diminished passer Cέβψ┬░ between C and Dm chromatic stepping-stone between diatonic chords Tritone sub Dέβφ7 instead of G7 jazz swap έΑΦ same tritone engine, chromatic bass fall Worked example | C | E7 | Am | C7 | I V7/vi vi V7/IV έΗΡ the E7 and C7 are visitors: | F | Fm | C G | C | each one a dominant aimed IV iv I V I at the NEXT chord borrowed! έΗΣ έΨ╢ Hear it (roots + pads) Listen for how E7 makes Am feel inevitable, and how Fm aches on the way home. That's function, audible. The details (open as much as you like) What analysis changes about your playing Tension chords get leading approaches (that's what they're FOR); home chords get planted roots; secondary dominants beg for chromatic walks into their targets; a tritone sub means your bass gets a chromatic descent for free (Dέβφ falling to C). Analysis isn't homework

h: The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 8 ┬╖ Expert Track Improvisation & fills Improvising isn't inventing from nothing έΑΦ it's recombining vocabulary you own, inside a form you trust, with taste as the filter. You already have all three pieces; this page teaches the recombining. Before you start Chord-tone fluency ( Unit 5 ), the pentatonic boxes , and a few stolen lines in the notebook. Fills: the gateway improvisation A fill is a one-or-two-beat decoration at a phrase boundary. The rules of the craft: Location: ends of 4- and 8-bar phrases, section seams, under held vocal notes έΑΦ the built-in parking spots. Aim: a fill is an approach note with ambition έΑΦ it must LAND (usually on the next downbeat's root). Material: pentatonic fragments, chord-tone runs, chromatic walks, one octave pop έΑΦ the vocabulary you have. Budget: one fill per phrase maximum. The bar you DON'T fill is what makes the one you do sound intentional. The fill workshop έΨ╢ Play all three Three shapes into the same Am landing: scale walk, pentatonic tumble, chromatic slide. Steal, then vary. Building a solo (yes, you) Bass solos are rarer than guitar solos, and better for it. The method that works the first time: sing a phrase, play what you sang, breathe, repeat. The voice can't play anything your taste doesn't approve, so the filter is built in. Structure it like speech: statement, variation, contrast, home έΑΦ over a form you know cold (the 12-bar is perfect). The details (open as much as you like) Motif thinking: one idea, four ways Great improvisers develop, not enumerate. Take three notes (say RέΑΥέβφ3έΑΥ4). Play them. Then: same rhythm higher; same notes different rhythm; backwards; leave a hole where a note was. One cell, one chorus of music έΑΦ and listeners hear intent instead of scales. Question & answer with yourself Two-bar question (ends up in the air έΑΦ off the root), two-bar answer (comes home). Loop the groove machine's vamp and hold this conversation for ten minutes. It's the fastest route to solos with shape έΑΦ and secretly it's
Eight bars looping (groove machine, any preset): solid line for 3┬╜ bars, ONE fill into bar 5's downbeat, repeat έΑΦ ten minutes, every fill landing.
One 12-bar chorus built phrase by phrase: sing two bars, play those two bars, until a complete sung-then-played solo exists έΑΦ taste guaranteed by your own voice.

h: The two ways to think about modes
Hear a mode change the same root
The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 8 ┬╖ Expert Track Modes έΑΦ seven flavors of one scale You met the idea in Unit 3: same notes, different home. Now make it practical έΑΦ because two of the seven modes (Dorian and Mixolydian) quietly run most of the funk, rock, and jam-band music you play. Before you start Major scale and relative thinking . From here on, standard terminology leads έΑΦ every term still taps. The two ways to think about modes Derivative (good for finding notes): D Dorian = the C major scale played from D. Parallel (good for hearing): D Dorian = D natural minor with the 6th raised. The first gets your fingers there; the second tells your ears what changed. Experts flip between both without noticing. The working bassist's mode table Mode Formula vs major Flavor ┬╖ where it lives Ionian (major) έΑΦ home base; pop, everything Dorian έβφ3 έβφ7 minor-but-hopeful; funk vamps, "So What", Santana Phrygian έβφ2 έβφ3 έβφ6 έβφ7 Spanish/metal darkness Lydian έβψ4 floating film-score major Mixolydian έβφ7 major with a blues collar; rock & dominant-7 vamps Aeolian (nat. minor) έβφ3 έβφ6 έβφ7 the default minor; you know it Locrian έβφ2 έβφ3 έβφ5 έβφ6 έβφ7 home of m7έβφ5; rarely a resting place Bold rows are the daily drivers. When a one-chord vamp is minor 7, think Dorian; when it's dominant 7, think Mixolydian έΑΦ those raised 6ths and lowered 7ths are what keep long vamps from going stale. Hear a mode change the same root Ionian Dorian Mixolydian Aeolian Phrygian All from root A έΑΦ the drone stays, the flavor moves. Press a mode to hear it over the root. The details (open as much as you like) When modes actually matter to a bassist Honestly: on vamps and modal tunes έΑΦ one chord for a long time, where the color notes you choose (the Dorian 6th, the Mixolydian έβφ7) ARE the harmony. On fast-changing progressions, chord tones + approach grammar matter far more than mode names. Modes are seasoning for harmonic standstill. Hearing them fast: the one-note trick Each daily-driver mode differs from plain major/minor by ONE note: Mixolydian = major

h: The one non-negotiable: play with people
Where to go deeper (a short, opinionated list)
b: Unit 8 ┬╖ Expert Track Next steps έΑΦ the map for the years after This site can take you from "which one is the E string?" to walking a jazz blues. What it can't do is be a band. Here's the honest map of what comes next, and how everything you built here keeps compounding. The one non-negotiable: play with people Every skill on this site exists for the moment another human counts to four. Nothing έΑΦ not one lab, not one exercise έΑΦ substitutes for it, and nothing accelerates you faster. Bassists have a structural advantage: everyone needs one . Realistic entry points: Jams and open mics έΑΦ blues jams especially: the form is the 12-bar you own, and "blues in A?" is a complete rehearsal. One other person έΑΦ a guitarist or pianist friend and a chart. Duos expose everything kindly. Community ensembles, church bands, school groups έΑΦ structured, forgiving, regular. Regular beats impressive. Online collaboration έΑΦ trading stems counts; timing against someone else's take is real ensemble skill. Your weekly diet (the maintenance version) Daily-ish: the routine builder keeps technique and repertoire honest in 20 minutes. Weekly: one steal ; a few minutes of ear trainer ; one groove-machine session in a style you're worst at. Monthly: record yourself playing a full song and listen like a producer. The recording is still the honest mirror. Where to go deeper (a short, opinionated list) Reading: Standing in the Shadows of Motown (Jamerson transcriptions έΑΦ a bible), Ed Friedland's Building Walking Bass Lines , Rufus Reid's The Evolving Bassist (upright perspective that improves electric players). Listening as study: pick one bassist per month and binge them: Jamerson έΗΤ Jaco έΗΤ Duck Dunn έΗΤ Carol Kaye έΗΤ Paul McCartney έΗΤ Bootsy έΗΤ Pino Palladino έΗΤ Esperanza Spalding. Steal one thing from each. A teacher, occasionally: even two or three lessons a year from a good teacher catches habits no website can see. Bring them a recording of yourself; that's where lessons start mattering. What "expert"

h: The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 8 ┬╖ Expert Track Transcription έΑΦ stealing like a musician Lifting lines off records by ear is the single highest-value practice habit in existence: it trains ears, hands, vocabulary, and feel in one activity. Here's the method that makes it merely hard instead of impossible. Before you start Some ear-trainer mileage έΑΦ intervals at ~80% έΑΦ and the fretboard map . The method Choose winnable battles : clear mixes, moderate tempos, lines you already half-hum. (Motown > metal for audibility.) Loop tiny chunks έΑΦ two bars at most. Any looping player works; slow-downers (browser extensions, most streaming apps' 0.75├Ω) are legal performance-enhancers. Sing it first. If you can't sing the chunk, you haven't heard it yet έΑΦ keep looping BEFORE touching the bass. Find the first note (hunt on the neck), then use interval ears for the rest: "up a fourthέΑο down a stepέΑο". Get the rhythm honest : count it out loud against the record; ghost notes and rests are part of the line. Write it down έΑΦ tab, notation, or your own scrawl. Writing is what moves it from "solved" to "owned". Play along until invisible : your line and the record become one sound. That's the rep that counts. The details (open as much as you like) A graded starter list Level 1 (roots, clear): Bill Withers "Lean On Me", Neil Young "Harvest Moon". Level 2 (moving lines): "Stand By Me", "My Girl", "With or Without You". Level 3 (vocabulary): "What's Going On", "Come Together", "Money". Level 4 (grids & ghosts): "Sir Duke", "What Is Hip?", anything Jamerson that scares you. One song at your edge beats five beneath it. When you're stuck on a note Check the octave (bass fundamentals fool ears έΑΦ try the same fret 12 higher); check for open strings (that resonance is a fingerprint); ask the harmony (it's probably a chord tone έΑΦ which chord is passing?); and suspect ghost notes when the rhythm has more attacks than pitches. Still stuck? Move on and return έΑΦ ears grow overnight, literally. Tab sites vs. your ears Checking a
One Level-1/2 song lifted completely by ear, written down in any notation, and played along with the record until the two blur.
Four consecutive weeks, one transcribed line (or riff, or fill) per week έΑΦ logged here έΑΦ until stealing is just something you do.

t: Session 1 ┬╖ Day one: make it rumble
b: Session 1 ┬╖ Phase 1 έΑΦ First sounds Day one: make it rumble Bass in hand? Good. In the next twenty minutes you'll make your first sounds, tune them by ear, and play a real four-string bass line with a steady pulse. Reading about music starts later έΑΦ today you play. Get ready One minute, tops έΑΦ then you're playing. Sit or stand έΑΦ your call. If you have a strap, set it so the bass hangs at the same height either way; future-you will be glad the two feel identical. Body against you, neck angled slightly up. Sitting: the bass's waist rests on your right thigh. Right forearm rests on the top edge of the body, hand hanging relaxed over the strings. Left hand cradles the neck έΑΦ thumb on the back, fingers curled loosely over the strings. It does nothing else today. No amp? No problem. Unplugged is plenty loud for everything in this session. Playing left-handed? Mirror everything, today and always. Go deeper: the parts of the bass, and holding it well έΗΤ Make your first sound Rest your right thumb on the pickup έΑΦ the plastic-looking bar sitting under the strings. Now put the side of your index fingertip against the thickest string , and pull across it, toward the floor-side string έΑΦ not up and away. Let it ring. That low rumble is the open E έΑΦ the lowest note your bass has, and the foundation half the songs you know are standing on. Tune up You've made four sounds έΑΦ now make them the right four sounds. Tap a pad, play the matching string, and turn that string's peg a little at a time until the two sounds melt into one. Tighter = higher, looser = lower. Can't tell higher from lower yet? Tune your string clearly too low, then come up slowly until the wobble dies. Every bassist started there. Go deeper: tuning, beats, and the 5th-fret self-check έΗΤ ┬╖ or use the microphone tuner Your first bass line Four strings, one pattern, still no left hand. Count "1 2 3 4" out loud έΑΦ one pluck per count, and change strings each time you say "1". Why that worked You just played the four open st

t: Practice: Four strings, one at a time
b: Each open string rings long and clean έΑΦ no buzz, no double-hit έΑΦ and you name it as it sounds.
Sixteen even plucks έΑΦ a bar each on E, A, D, then A έΑΦ with every string change landing exactly on the count of 1.

t: Session 2 ┬╖ Two fingers, one steady beat
b: Session 2 ┬╖ Phase 1 έΑΦ First sounds Two fingers, one steady beat Yesterday you plucked however your hand felt like it. Today your plucking hand gets its engine έΑΦ two fingers taking turns έΑΦ and the metronome stops being a button and becomes a bandmate. Tune up First thing, every time έΑΦ thirty seconds now buys honest feedback all session. Warm-up: the four-string tour Session 1's riff, once through, counting out loud. Care about the change landing on "1" more than about speed. έΨ╢ Hear it New thing: the two-finger engine Anchor your thumb on the pickup. Now pluck the open E with your index finger, then your middle finger, then index, then middle έΑΦ like walking, finger after finger. Each fingertip pulls across the string and comes to rest against your thumb-sideέΑο and that's the whole technique. Tap έβσ 70 in the bar below and make every pluck land exactly on a click. Why that worked Two fingers taking strict turns is called alternation , and it's the engine behind almost everything a fingerstyle bassist plays: one finger doing all the work hits a speed ceiling in days; two never do. Plucking across the string (a rest stroke , landing on the next string) is what makes the note thick instead of twangy. And the number on the metronome? Tempo , in beats per minute έΑΦ 70 means seventy clicks each minute, and your job is to sit so exactly on them that the click seems to disappear into your note. Players call that swallowing the click . Which finger starts? And where should the thumb go later? Nobody checks which finger starts έΑΦ pick whichever, then alternate strictly even when a pattern would be easier repeating one finger; that's what builds the reflex. The thumb anchored on the pickup is the day-one home; later it learns to float onto the E string to quiet it while you play higher strings. That trick έΑΦ and where exactly to pluck for fat vs. punchy έΑΦ lives in the right-hand page below. Go deeper: the right hand έΗΤ ┬╖ timing & groove έΗΤ Apply it: cross the strings Same engine, now to

t: Practice: The two-finger engine, on one string
b: A full minute of open-E quarter notes on the click έΑΦ index-middle-index-middle without breaking pattern, and no pluck louder than its neighbors.
The tour of all four strings with alternation surviving every crossing έΑΦ whichever finger is due next takes the new string, no double-plucks at the seams.

h: New thing: press your first fret
Apply it: the bass checks its own tuning
b: Session 3 ┬╖ Phase 1 έΑΦ First sounds First frets, first landmarks Your left hand has been politely holding the neck for two sessions. Today it presses down έΑΦ three notes on one string, a riff that actually sounds like a riff, and a trick that lets the bass tune itself. Tune up Warm-up: the engine, crossing strings One pass of Session 2's crossing drill έΑΦ say "i, m" under your breath and don't let the alternation reset at the seams. έΨ╢ Hear it New thing: press your first fret Look at the metal strips crossing the neck. Put a left-hand fingertip on the thickest string just behind the third strip έΑΦ close behind it, not on top of it έΑΦ and press with the finger's tip, arm hanging relaxed. Pluck. That clear note is a G . Slide up to just behind the fifth strip: that's an A . Any finger that reaches comfortably is the right finger today έΑΦ fingering systems come later. Why that worked Each metal strip is a fret , and each fret raises the string by exactly one semitone έΑΦ the smallest step in Western music. Open E, count three frets up: E έΗΤ F έΗΤ Fέβψ έΗΤ G . Two more: A . The whole neck is just that one rule, repeated. The dots on frets 3, 5, 7 (then 9 and the double-dot 12) aren't decoration έΑΦ they're the landmarks every bassist navigates by, and you just played two of them. Tap anywhere έΑΦ every fret plays its note. Find G and A on the E string, then peek at fret 5 against the open A above it. Why the frets get closer together up the neck Each semitone multiplies the string's vibrating speed by the same amount (about 1.06├Ω), so each fret has to shave off the same fraction of string έΑΦ and a fraction of an ever-shorter string is an ever-smaller slice. Equal steps of pitch, shrinking steps of wood. The full math lives in strings & fret math . Go deeper: the left hand έΗΤ Apply it: the bass checks its own tuning Here's the landmark trick: fret 5 of any string sounds the same note as the next open string . E at fret 5 is an A έΑΦ the very note the open A string plays. If the pair wobbles, on
The riff loops with every fretted note ringing clear έΑΦ no buzz, no thud έΑΦ and your left hand staying relaxed enough to wave hello between bars.
Each fret-5 note and its neighboring open string ring as one sound έΑΦ and when a pair wobbles, you know which peg to reach for.

h: New thing: hold the floor under two chords
b: Session 4 ┬╖ Phase 1 έΑΦ First sounds Your first bass line Riffs are fun; a bass line has a job. Today you hold down two chords the way a working bassist does, learn the job description, and discover you already played a three-chord song in Session 1 without knowing it. Tune up Bonus points: check E against A with the fret-5 trick from last time before you touch the pads. Warm-up: the box riff Session 3's EέΑΥGέΑΥA riff, twice through, left hand staying soft. έΨ╢ Hear it New thing: hold the floor under two chords Imagine a guitarist swaying between an E chord and an A chord, four beats each. Your job: play the chord's name-note έΑΦ E under the E chord, A under the A chord έΑΦ and land the change exactly with them. One long note per chord is a complete, professional bass line. Really. Tap έΨ╢ on the card to hear the chords ring over your two notes. Why that worked The note a chord is named after is its root , and playing roots on the beat is the bassist's actual job description: you're the floor the chords stand on and the pulse the band walks to έΑΦ harmony's anchor and rhythm's anchor, in one instrument . That's why one whole note, placed exactly, beats a flurry of clever ones. Notice what mattered: the moment of the change . Nail the root on beat 1 of each new chord and everything sounds intentional; smear it and even right notes sound wrong. Will it always be the root? (a peek ahead) No έΑΦ thirds, fifths and walking lines are coming. But the pros' secret is that even the fanciest line usually touches the root exactly where the ear needs the floor: beat 1, chord change. Learn the plain version so deeply that the fancy versions stay honest. The full argument lives in the bassist's job . Go deeper: the role of the bass έΗΤ ┬╖ roots & chord tones έΗΤ Apply it: the three-chord song Now add D. E for a bar, A for a bar, D for a bar, back to A έΑΦ in quarter notes it's exactly the four-string tour from Session 1 . You've been playing a song all along; now you know why it works. Push it: whole no
Eight bars of E and A in whole notes έΑΦ every change landing dead on beat 1, every note held its full four counts, no gap and no early jump.
EέΑΥAέΑΥDέΑΥA in steady quarters with chord changes announced cleanly on every beat 1 έΑΦ the four-string tour, now played as harmony on purpose.

Session 5 ┬╖ Phase 1 έΑΦ First sounds Count it out loud You've been feeling wholes, quarters and doubled-up notes for four sessions. Today they get their names, and you get the counting language έΑΦ spoken out loud, yes, actually out loud έΑΦ that lets you play a rhythm you've never heard. Tune up Warm-up: the three-chord song Session 4's EέΑΥAέΑΥDέΑΥA, one pass in quarters. Say the chord names on the changes. έΨ╢ Hear it New thing: the value ladder One note έΑΦ open A έΑΦ four rhythms. Each bar holds the same four beats; the notes just slice them differently. Count out loud exactly as written under the notes: numbers on the beats, "&" (say "and") between them. Why that worked Every bar in 4/4 holds four beats , and note values just say how many of them a note keeps: a whole note keeps all four, a half keeps two, a quarter keeps one, and an eighth splits the beat in two έΑΦ the doubled-up feel you found in Session 2, now with a name. Counting aloud works because your mouth is a better metronome than your mind: thinking drifts, speech doesn't. Every serious player counted aloud for years έΑΦ the ones who skip it are the ones who rush. Dots and ties, in one breath A dot after a note stretches it by half again έΑΦ a dotted half holds three beats. A tie (a curved line joining two notes) glues their lengths into one sound, which is how written music holds a note across a barline. You'll read both constantly; the full tour with played examples is in rhythm & counting . Go deeper: rhythm & counting έΗΤ Apply it: holes and holds Real bass lines are made of notes and silences. A rest is a silence with a length έΑΦ you count it, you just don't play it. Mute the string with a flat left hand the moment the rest starts. Bar 3 rests on beat 1 έΑΦ the hardest beat to not play. Count louder there. (Reggae builds a whole style on that hole; you'll meet it in Session 28.) Quick check Wrap up Phase 1 complete. You tune by ear, your right hand has an engine, your left hand has landmarks, you know the job, and you sp
All four bars flow without a stumble while you count "1 & 2 & 3 & 4 &" out loud the whole way έΑΦ the eighth-note bar strictly alternated.
Rests land as clean, counted silences έΑΦ string muted dead, no ring-over έΑΦ and the dotted half truly holds through beat 3.

t: Session 6 ┬╖ The fretboard is a machine
b: Session 6 ┬╖ Phase 2 έΑΦ Fretboard & rhythm The fretboard is a machine Ninety-six places to put a finger looks like chaos. It's actually a machine with three rules έΑΦ and once your hands know them, "where is C?" stops being a memory question and becomes a one-second calculation. Tune up Warm-up: the box riff Session 3's EέΑΥGέΑΥA riff, one relaxed pass έΑΦ those two dots at frets 3 and 5 are about to grow into a whole navigation system. έΨ╢ Hear it New thing: the octave jump Play open E. Now go two strings up (to the D string) and two frets up (fret 2) έΑΦ play that. Same note, higher floor: an octave. That two-up-two-over jump keeps its shape everywhere on the neck . Try it from G (fret 3) and A (fret 5). Why that worked The machine's three rules: Rule 1 έΑΦ each string is tuned five frets (a fourth) above its neighbor, which is why fret 5 always equals the next open string. Rule 2 έΑΦ twelve frets up, the alphabet wraps: fret 12 is the same letter as the open string, one octave higher (that's the double dot). Rule 3 έΑΦ because rules 1 and 2 never change, every shape you learn works from any starting note. The octave jump is just rule 1 twice (10 frets of pitch) plus 2 frets. Tap around the map έΑΦ every E, A and D under fret 12 is marked: Notice the landmarks: dots at 3έΑΥ5έΑΥ7έΑΥ9, double at 12. Low-string names at those dots (GέΑΥAέΑΥB on E; CέΑΥDέΑΥE on A) unlock almost every root you'll need for months. One sound, two names? The note between F and G answers to Fέβψ ("F sharp έΑΦ F raised a fret") and Gέβφ ("G flat έΑΦ G lowered a fret"). Same fret, same sound, two spellings; which name is "right" depends on the key you're in έΑΦ that story starts in Session 12. Until then: sharps going up, flats coming down is a fine habit. Full tour in notes on the fretboard . Go deeper: strings & fret math έΗΤ ┬╖ notes on the fretboard έΗΤ Apply it: the note hunt Use the rules, not memory: fret 5 = next open string, twelve = wrap, octave shape = two-and-two. Say each note's name as you land on it. Then make it a sport: the

t: Practice: Octave jumps from three landmarks
b: E, G and A each jump cleanly to their octave έΑΦ two strings up, two frets over έΑΦ with both notes ringing evenly and the low note damped once you land.

t: Practice: Find every A, then every D
b: Every A below fret 12 in one unbroken pass, names said aloud έΑΦ then every D έΑΦ using the rules (fret 5, fret 12, octave shape) instead of staring.

t: Session 7 ┬╖ Read it: tab and staff
h: New thing: play from the numbers
Apply it: now read the dots
b: Session 7 ┬╖ Phase 2 έΑΦ Fretboard & rhythm Read it: tab and staff Bass has two written languages: tab's honest little numbers and the staff's floating dots. Today you play the same riff from both έΑΦ and learn which language tells you what. Tune up Warm-up: octave jumps Two rounds of Session 6's E and G jumps έΑΦ shape as one hand-frame, names out loud. έΨ╢ Hear it New thing: play from the numbers Look at the four-line grid under the notation έΑΦ that's tab . Four lines = your four strings, thickest E on the bottom . A number is a fret on that string; 0 means open. Read only the numbers for now and let your ear supply the rhythm from the έΨ╢ button. Why that worked The two systems split the truth between them. Tab tells your hands where έΑΦ string and fret, no ambiguity, which matters on bass because the same note lives in several places. The staff tells your ears what έΑΦ pitch and rhythm. Bass reads the bass clef (the fancy "9" whose two dots hug the F line ): lines GέΑΥBέΑΥDέΑΥFέΑΥA bottom to top, spaces AέΑΥCέΑΥEέΑΥG. Your open strings on the staff: E hangs below on a ledger line, A sits under the bottom line, D takes the middle line, G the fourth line. Learn those four addresses and you can triangulate everything else. The internet-tab warning Most tab sites skip rhythm entirely έΑΦ numbers with no lengths. Fine when you already know how the song goes; useless when you don't. That's why real charts pair tab with notation (like this site does), and why the staff is worth befriending even if tab stays your first language. Full comparison in tab έΗΦ notation . Go deeper: the staff & bass clef έΗΤ ┬╖ tab έΗΦ notation έΗΤ Apply it: now read the dots A new phrase. This time work top-down: read the staff first έΑΦ name each note from its line or space, find it on the neck yourself έΑΦ and only then check your answer against the tab below it. Cover the tab line with a finger on the first pass. Honesty now = fluency later. Quick check Wrap up You can now be handed music and play it έΑΦ numbers for the hands, dots fo
The riff played straight off the numbers έΑΦ string changes and frets found from the tab alone, at a tempo where you never stop moving.

t: Practice: The answer, from the staff
b: Each note named from its line or space before your finger moves έΑΦ tab used only to check, never to lead.

h: New thing: the three-chord song, waltzing
b: Session 8 ┬╖ Phase 2 έΑΦ Fretboard & rhythm Meters you'll actually meet Every bar so far held four beats. Today you play the same song with three, then with 6/8's two big lopes έΑΦ and feel why the number at the front of the staff changes how your whole body counts. Tune up Warm-up: the climb Session 7's riff έΑΦ read it off the tab one more time, counting "1 2 3 4". έΨ╢ Hear it New thing: the three-chord song, waltzing Same EέΑΥAέΑΥDέΑΥA song as Session 4 έΑΦ but now each bar holds three beats. Count " 1 2 3" with a fat accent on the 1, and feel how the music starts to sway instead of march. Why that worked The fraction at the start of the staff is the time signature : the top number says how many beats fill a bar , the bottom says which note value counts as one beat . 4/4 έΑΦ four quarters έΑΦ is so standard it's literally called common time. 3/4 is the waltz: three quarters, heavy downbeat , sway. What changed for you as a bassist? Only the count. The job έΑΦ root on the 1, pulse locked έΑΦ survives every meter . 6/8 vs 3/4 έΑΦ the classic trap Both contain six eighth notes, so on paper they look like twins. The difference is grouping : 3/4 is three steady quarter beats (ONE-two-three), 6/8 is two big lopes of three eighths each (ONE-and-a-TWO-and-a). Count a 6/8 song in 3/4 and you'll fight the drummer all night. The details έΑΦ plus what the bassist does in each έΑΦ are in time signatures . Go deeper: time signatures έΗΤ Apply it: the 6/8 lope Now feel the twin that isn't: six eighths grouped as two pulses. Count " 1 -2-3 4 -5-6", leaning on 1 and 4 έΑΦ your hand rolls EέΑΥGέΑΥA up and down like a slow tide. Same three notes as the box riff έΑΦ only the meter is new. That's deliberate: change one thing at a time. Quick check Wrap up March, waltz, lope έΑΦ three body-feels, one job. When a chart says 3/4 or 6/8 you now know what your count does before a note is played. Next time: the five little shapes that turn the fretboard into a map of sounds έΑΦ intervals, the atoms of everything melodic. Mark Session
EέΑΥAέΑΥDέΑΥA in 3/4 with an audible lean on every "1" έΑΦ three even beats, no phantom fourth sneaking in at the barline.
Six eighths rolling as TWO pulses έΑΦ the lean lands on 1 and 4, and a listener would sway, not march.

t: Session 9 ┬╖ Interval shapes and their sounds
h: Apply it: hum first, then play
b: Session 9 ┬╖ Phase 2 έΑΦ Fretboard & rhythm Interval shapes and their sounds Five small hand-shapes, one bonus reach έΑΦ that's the entire melodic alphabet of the bass. Learn each shape's sound today and you'll never again wonder what a line will sound like before you play it. Tune up Warm-up: the 6/8 lope One pass of last session's lope έΑΦ lean on 1 and 4, let it sway. έΨ╢ Hear it New thing: the shape tour Home base: A at fret 5 of the E string . Every pair in this tour starts there and reaches for one shape. Play each pair slowly, twice, and listen for its personality έΑΦ solid, bright, hollow, sweet, sad, bluesy. Why that worked The distance between two notes is an interval , measured in semitones έΑΦ and on bass, every interval is a shape that never changes. Next string same fret: a fourth (5 semitones, open and sturdy). Next string two frets up: a fifth (7 έΑΦ the power-chord sound). Two strings and two frets: the octave (12 έΑΦ "same but higher"). Next string one fret back : a major third (4, sweet); two back: a minor third (3, sad). Bonus reach έΑΦ two strings straight up: a minor seventh (10, the funky one). Because the neck's rules never change, the shape is the sound. Grab any root, make the fifth shape, and you already know what you'll hear. All six targets from the A root. Tap them; say the interval, then the note name. Why "major", "minor", "perfect"? Thirds, sixths and sevenths come in two sizes έΑΦ the bigger is "major", the smaller "minor". Fourths, fifths and octaves come in one size so pure the old theorists called them "perfect". You don't need the full taxonomy yet; your ear is collecting the sounds, which is the part that can't be crammed later. The complete tour έΑΦ all thirteen intervals with quality names έΑΦ is in intervals . Go deeper: intervals as shapes έΗΤ ┬╖ intervals έΗΤ Apply it: hum first, then play The shapes only pay off when the sound arrives before the finger. Reverse the flow: play the root, hum where you think the interval lands, then play the second note

t: Practice: The shape tour from A
b: All six pairs played clean from the same A root, and you can say each shape's name and personality before its second note sounds.
For fourths, fifths and octaves: your hummed guess lands on (or a hair from) the real note at least four times out of five.

Session 10 ┬╖ Phase 2 έΑΦ Fretboard & rhythm One finger per fret So far your left hand has been improvising its fingering. Today it gets a system έΑΦ four fingers, four frets, one job each έΑΦ plus the two little moves (hammer and pull) that let notes sing without a pluck. Tune up Warm-up: the shape tour, abridged From A: fourth, fifth, octave έΑΦ say the names, hear the personalities. έΨ╢ Hear it New thing: the spider Park your hand so your index hovers over fret 5 έΑΦ then middle owns 6, ring owns 7, pinky owns 8. That's the whole system: one finger per fret , nobody moonlights. Crawl it slowly across all four strings. Why that worked A four-fret window with a finger assigned to each fret is a position έΑΦ named after the index finger's fret, so you just played "5th position". Inside a position, every note is one finger-drop away: no searching, no stretching, no wasted motion. Most bass lines live inside one position for bars at a time; when the music leaves the window, you don't reach έΑΦ you shift , sliding the whole frame as one unit ( shifting ). Here's the payoff έΑΦ the Session 7 climb, written twice: once around open position, once entirely inside 5th position. Same notes, two homes: έΨ╢ Hear it Numbers under the notes are fingers (1 = index έΑο 4 = pinky). In the second pass A sits under 1, C under 4 on the same string, D under 1 on the next έΑΦ one frame, zero travel. The quiet half of technique: muting Great bassists are defined less by the notes they start than the ones they stop. Fingers that aren't fretting rest lightly on the strings; the right thumb quiets the E string when you play higher έΑΦ both hands share the silence work. It develops naturally if you listen for ring-over; the full checklist is in the left hand . Go deeper: positions & fingering έΗΤ ┬╖ the left hand έΗΤ Apply it: hammer-ons and pull-offs Two notes, one pluck: pluck A (index, fret 5) and hammer the ring finger down hard onto fret 7 έΑΦ the B sounds by impact alone. Reverse it: with both fingers down, pluck, then
Frets 5-6-7-8 on every string with the assigned fingers only έΑΦ each note clean, hand frame still, thumb loose behind the neck.
The hammered and pulled notes speak as loudly as the plucked ones έΑΦ one pluck per pair, no volume drop on the second note.

t: Session 11 ┬╖ The major scale under your hand
h: New thing: the G major box
b: Session 11 ┬╖ Phase 3 έΑΦ Theory in your hands The major scale under your hand Do-re-mi lives in a two-and-a-half-fret box, and your hand already has the finger system to play it. Learn the box once and you own it in every key έΑΦ that's today's whole trick. Tune up Warm-up: the spider, briefly One trip up frets 2-3-4-5 on the E and A strings έΑΦ the box you're about to learn lives right there. έΨ╢ Hear it New thing: the G major box Start on G (E string, fret 3, middle finger ). Follow the tab exactly έΑΦ the fingering under each note is the pattern your hand will keep forever. Go up, come back down, and sing the numbers "1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8" as you go. Yes, out loud; you know the drill. Why that worked You just played the major scale έΑΦ the do-re-mi your ear has known since childhood. Its recipe is a fixed chain of whole and half steps: W-W-H-W-W-W-H , which on bass is just frets: +2 +2 +1 +2 +2 +2 +1 . Start anywhere, follow the recipe, get sunshine. The numbers you sang are degrees έΑΦ they're how musicians name notes relative to home , and they're about to become your main language: "play the 5" works in every key, "play D" only works in one. The whole neck in G major έΑΦ your one-octave box is the bottom corner of this map. Roots are solid; tap around, nothing here sounds wrong together. The two half steps are the flavor Only two neighbors sit one fret apart: 3έΗΤ4 and 7έΗΤ8. Those two "leaners" are where the scale's pull lives έΑΦ hang on 7 and feel it ache toward 8. Every melody trick and every mode you'll ever meet comes down to where those half steps land; the full story is in the major scale . Go deeper: the major scale έΗΤ Apply it: move the box The shape is movable έΑΦ that's the superpower. Slide the whole pattern up two frets and it's A major . Start it from fret 8 and it's C major . Same fingers, same recipe, new key. Test yourself: hum "do" on any random fret, play the box from it, and hear do-re-mi appear under your hand. Quick check Wrap up One box, one recipe, every key. h: New thing: move up a fifth, listen for the click
Apply it: walk the circle on the neck
b: Session 12 ┬╖ Phase 3 έΑΦ Theory in your hands Keys and the circle Move yesterday's box up a fifth and exactly one note changes. Do that twelve times and you've walked through every key in music and back home έΑΦ that walk has a name, and today you play it. Tune up Warm-up: the G box One octave of G major, up and down, degrees sung. It's the reference point for everything today. έΨ╢ Hear it New thing: move up a fifth, listen for the click Play the G box. Now start the same shape from D (A string, fret 5) and listen hard to the 7th degree . In G major that note was C; in D major it's Cέβψ έΑΦ one semitone, one single changed note, and everything else survives the move. Why that worked A key is a home note plus its major-scale family. Move home up a fifth and the recipe forces exactly one resident to sharpen (the new 7th). Move up another fifth, one more sharp. Arrange all twelve homes by fifths and you get the circle of fifths έΑΦ music's subway map. Neighbors share six of seven notes; opposite sides barely speak. The key signature έΑΦ that cluster of sharps or flats at the start of a staff έΑΦ is just the changed residents listed once, so the page doesn't nag you note by note. Tap any key έΑΦ majors outside, relative minors inside. Each tap shows the signature and plays the root. And the flat direction? Walk the circle the other way έΑΦ down a fifth (or up a fourth, same thing) έΑΦ and instead of gaining sharps you gain flats: F has one, Bέβφ two, Eέβφ threeέΑο Bass players live on this side of the circle more than most, because horns love flat keys. Deep dive with the interactive signature builder in keys & the circle of fifths . Go deeper: keys & the circle of fifths έΗΤ Apply it: walk the circle on the neck Here's the beautiful part: "up a fifth" is a shape you already own έΑΦ next string, two frets up (Session 9). So the circle isn't abstract: it's a physical walk your hand can do. Say each root as you land. CέΑΥGέΑΥDέΑΥAέΑΥE one way, CέΑΥFέΑΥBέβφέΑΥEέβφ the other έΑΦ those eight cover ninety percent of the songs

t: Practice: G box, D box έΑΦ spot the changed note
b: Both scales fluent, and you can point at the one note that changed (CέΗΤCέβψ) and hear the difference it makes on the 7th degree's lean.
Roots around both sides of the circle from memory έΑΦ sharps side C-G-D-A-E, flats side C-F-Bέβφ-Eέβφ έΑΦ each move played as its fifth/fourth shape, names said aloud.

h: New thing: the A minor box
Apply it: flip a song's mood
b: Session 13 ┬╖ Phase 3 έΑΦ Theory in your hands Minor: the other home Every major key hides a moodier twin inside it έΑΦ same seven notes, different gravity. Today you play both twins, flip a song's mood with one fret, and find out your Session 1 riff was minor all along. Tune up Warm-up: the circle walk Sharp side up, flat side back έΑΦ root names out loud. έΨ╢ Hear it New thing: the A minor box Start on A (E string, fret 5, index this time) and follow the tab. Every note in this scale also lives in C major έΑΦ yet it doesn't sound like sunshine at all, because home has moved to A. Why that worked That was the natural minor scale έΑΦ recipe W-H-W-W-H-W-W (frets +2 +1 +2 +2 +1 +2 +2). A minor and C major hold identical notes; each is the other's relative . What changed is gravity : phrases now rest on A, and the little 3rd degree έΑΦ C, a minor third above home, your "sad" shape from Session 9 έΑΦ colors everything. Different question, different twin: keep the same home and just swap the scale's mood έΑΦ A major versus A minor έΑΦ and that pair is called parallel . Relative = same notes, new home. Parallel = same home, new notes. Three minors in one breath Natural minor is the raw material. Harmonic minor raises the 7th one fret so it leans home hard (instant Bond-villain flavor έΑΦ you'll taste it in the exercise). Melodic minor smooths the resulting gap for rising melodies. Bassists mostly live in natural minor; knowing why the other two exist is enough for now έΑΦ the full story is in minor scales . Go deeper: minor scales έΗΤ Apply it: flip a song's mood The fastest ear-training on bass: change one note έΑΦ the third έΑΦ and flip a line from major to minor. Then taste harmonic minor's raised 7th. And yes: EέΑΥGέΑΥA, your day-one riff? That's the spine of E minor . You've been a minor-key player since Session 1. Quick check Wrap up Two homes, two twins (relative and parallel), and a one-fret mood switch under your fingers. Your ear now files every song it hears into one of two boxes έΑΦ that's a supe
One octave of A natural minor up and down, degrees sung ("1 2 έβφ3 4 5 έβφ6 έβφ7 8"), with the sad third landing dead in tune.

t: Practice: One fret flips the mood
b: The major and minor versions of the same line played back to back until the flip is obvious before it happens έΑΦ then the harmonic-minor bar with its raised 7th leaning hard on home.

h: New thing: the E minor pentatonic box έΑΦ free with your bass
b: Session 14 ┬╖ Phase 3 έΑΦ Theory in your hands Pentatonics: five safe notes Subtract the two touchiest notes from a scale and what's left can't play a wrong note. Today you learn the box every rock and funk bassist lives in έΑΦ and then you improvise for the first time. Yes, you. Tune up Warm-up: the mood flip A major climb, A minor climb, and the harmonic-minor lean έΑΦ ears first, then fingers. έΨ╢ Hear it New thing: the E minor pentatonic box έΑΦ free with your bass Five notes: E ┬╖ G ┬╖ A ┬╖ B ┬╖ D . On this instrument they practically play themselves έΑΦ two open strings, three easy frets, no stretch anywhere. Learn it up and down until your hand stops thinking. Why that worked The pentatonic scale is a seven-note scale with the two leaners removed έΑΦ the half-step neighbors that demand careful handling. E natural minor drops its 2nd and έβφ6th, leaving 1-έβφ3-4-5-έβφ7: five notes with no half step anywhere , so nothing clashes hard against the chord. That's why it feels safe έΑΦ not dumbed down, just de-mined. The box you played is the open-position corner of this map. Same five notes everywhere έΑΦ the dots are your playground. The blue note Add one forbidden fruit έΑΦ the έβφ5, Bέβφ in E's case έΑΦ and minor pentatonic becomes the blues scale . It's a passing sting, not a parking spot: slide through it on the way from A to B and you'll hear fifty years of blues in one fret. More (plus major pentatonic, its sunny cousin) in pentatonics & blues . Go deeper: pentatonics & the blues scale έΗΤ Apply it: your first improvisation Time to make something up. Start the band, stay inside the five notes, and follow the three rules on the card. There are no wrong notes in the box έΑΦ only phrases you haven't finished yet. Your backing band: groove machine έΑΦ E vamp, rock 8ths, 90 bpm . Keep it running and come back to this card. Quick check Wrap up Five safe notes, one sting, and your first improvised phrases over a groove. The box will be your safety net for the rest of your playing life έΑΦ and it cost you one

t: Practice: The open E minor pentatonic box
b: Up and down at 90 without looking έΑΦ E G A B D E flowing as one gesture, open strings ringing as long as the fretted notes.

t: Practice: First improv: three rules, five notes
b: Two full minutes of made-up phrases over the vamp without stopping έΑΦ phrases that breathe (play a bit, rest a bit) and land on E more often than not.

t: Session 15 ┬╖ Triads: chords one note at a time
h: New thing: major, minor, diminished έΑΦ three arpeggio shapes
Apply it: upgrade the three-chord song
b: Session 15 ┬╖ Phase 3 έΑΦ Theory in your hands Triads: chords one note at a time A chord is three notes stacked; a bassist plays them one at a time and calls it an arpeggio. Learn three small shapes today and your root-only lines suddenly get a vocabulary. Tune up Warm-up: the pentatonic box E minor pentatonic up and down, eyes closed by the third loop. έΨ╢ Hear it New thing: major, minor, diminished έΑΦ three arpeggio shapes All from the same A root. Major: root, third up one string one fret back, fifth up one string two frets on έΑΦ the happy stack. Minor: same but the third drops a fret. Diminished: minor with the fifth squeezed down a fret too. Play each slowly and name what you hear: open, sad, clenched. Why that worked A triad is degrees 1-3-5 of a scale sounded together; play them in sequence and it's an arpeggio . The third sets the mood (major vs minor έΑΦ you knew that already), the fifth adds stability, and the root names the whole thing. Those three notes are the chord tones έΑΦ the notes that can't be wrong while their chord is sounding, which makes them a bassist's ammunition, ranked: root strongest, fifth safest, third most colorful . Why diminished sounds "clenched" Stack two minor thirds and the outer interval is a tritone έΑΦ six semitones, the most restless sound in the system. Diminished triads live on the major scale's 7th degree, wanting resolution. You'll rarely park on one, but you'll pass through them έΑΦ details in triads . Go deeper: triads έΗΤ ┬╖ roots & chord tones έΗΤ Apply it: upgrade the three-chord song Session 4's EέΑΥAέΑΥD song, three energy levels: roots only (you own this), then rootέΑΥfifth (the country/rock workhorse), then the full rootέΑΥthirdέΑΥfifth climb. Feel your line get richer while the job stays the same. RootέΑΥfifth secret: the fifth also lives below the root έΑΦ same fret, one string down. Low fifths sound huge. Quick check Wrap up Three shapes, a ranked ammunition list, and a bass line that grew from one note to three without losing its job. This is
Major, minor and diminished arpeggios from A, up and back, each shape's sound nameable with eyes closed: open, sad, clenched.

t: Practice: The three-chord song, three energy levels
b: EέΑΥAέΑΥDέΑΥA played three ways without stopping έΑΦ roots, root-fifth, root-third-fifth έΑΦ changes still landing dead on beat 1 at every level.

t: Session 16 ┬╖ Seventh chords and chord symbols
h: New thing: the four seventh flavors
Apply it: play straight from symbols
b: Session 16 ┬╖ Phase 3 έΑΦ Theory in your hands Seventh chords and chord symbols Add one more note to the triad stack and chords learn to yearn, float and strut. Today you play the four flavors every chart is written in έΑΦ and start reading "Am7" and "G7" like short sentences. Tune up Warm-up: triads, three moods A major, A minor, A diminished arpeggios έΑΦ say the moods as you play. έΨ╢ Hear it New thing: the four seventh flavors Same A root, four stacks έΑΦ each one is a triad plus one more note on top. Play each arpeggio slowly and give the flavor a word of your own: dreamy? bluesy? mellow? anxious? The words you pick will stick better than anyone else's. Why that worked A seventh chord is 1-3-5 plus the 7th έΑΦ and the four combinations of major/minor third with major/minor seventh give the four flavors: maj7 (major 3rd + major 7th έΑΦ dreamy, at rest), 7 ("dominant" έΑΦ major 3rd + έβφ7 έΑΦ bluesy, pulling somewhere), m7 (minor 3rd + έβφ7 έΑΦ mellow, neutral), and m7έβφ5 (everything lowered έΑΦ anxious, wants out). And the symbols are grammar, not code: the capital letter is the root , then modifiers read left to right έΑΦ "m" makes the third minor, "7" adds the flat seventh, "maj7" adds the major one. C7 and Cmaj7 are different chords ; the word "maj" belongs to the seventh, not the triad. That one sentence prevents years of confusion. What the bassist actually plays over sevenths Rarely the whole stack. The workhorse is still root and fifth; the έβφ7 is the first color worth adding (rootέΑΥ5έΑΥέβφ7 outlines a dominant chord unmistakably); the third stays the mood-teller. Where each flavor lives inside a key έΑΦ and why the V chord is always a dominant 7 έΑΦ is next session's story, with the full tour in seventh chords . Go deeper: seventh chords & symbols έΗΤ Apply it: play straight from symbols A real chart moment: four symbols, no tab until you check. Read each symbol out loud in plain words first ("C major sevenέΑο A minor sevenέΑο"), find the root, then walk the arpeggio the chart implies. This progress
